data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: Move over, sons... Businesses run by parents and daughters on the rise
data.description:
For generations, British high streets have been lined with shops and businesses bearing the suffix “& sons”, denoting butchers, bakers and grocers handed down from father to son.

Now, women are taking the reins.

The number of businesses with names including the phrase “& daughters” has reached record levels since the turn of the century, new figures show.

There has been a 5,217 per cent increase in the use of the “& daughter” suffix by businesses since 2000, according to data uncovered by SumUp, a financial tech firm.

Rosie Dugard, 47, named her butchers Dugard & Daughters, in south London, after her two girls.

She told The Telegraph: “Why shouldn’t we celebrate our daughters? I think it’s amazing that more businesses are using the name ‘& daughters’.

“For me, the dream was always to run my own business. Even when I was a child and a teenager, I always had dreams of selling things that I had made.”

Sons still on top

Before the start of the 21st century, only 18 businesses used the phrase “& daughters”, compared to 1,184 businesses that were named using “& sons”.

SumUp also found that over the past five years, there was a 59 per cent rise in the number of “& daughter” registered companies.

But despite the sharp rise, the “& sons” suffix still outnumbers the female equivalent seven to one, with 7,527 businesses registered under the male version.

Mrs Dugard co-founded the female-led butchers with her husband Neil Dugard in 2013, when her daughters Pearl and Lily were aged three months and nine-years-old.

Now the girls are 13 and 16, and both have begun to work weekend shifts at the shop.

“Every other Sunday for the younger one and every Sunday for my eldest,” said Mrs Dugard, who set up the “dream business” after she and her husband were made redundant from their retail jobs.

Mrs Dugard said some older men appear “perplexed” when they enter their shops, as if struggling to cope with the idea of a butcher’s shop run by women.

She told The Telegraph that her female colleagues also get asked if they are the daughters, while her male colleagues are asked if the daughters name is a gimmick.

“Men from an older generation ask a lot of questions about who owns the business and assume that my husband owns the business,” she added.

‘Everyone is still surprised’

Twin sisters Hetty and Chelsea Stewart, 38, run a gutter-cleaning business named Stewart & Daughters.

Hetty Stewart said: “Everyone is still surprised when women turn up at the door; they always say they were not expecting a woman.

“It’s not in a bad way, they’re always really happy about it, they’re just shocked to see two women.”

She added: “I would want young girls to know that you are strong, you are capable, and you can do it, and people are not allowed to tell you otherwise.”

The twins, who took over the South Kensington business from their mother four years ago, said a plumber and a window cleaner had already asked their daughters to join the twins, so that they could see that women can work in male-dominated industries.

“Houseowners will run up and open the roof hatch for us, and you know they are just doing it because you are a woman,” said Hetty. “It’s a little bit condescending, but they’re not meaning to be.”

Chelsea Stewart said people assumed the business was male-led because of their surname, and that when their mother ran the business, people would also assume her male employees were in charge.

‘It makes me so happy and proud’

In the East Midlands, funeral director Inez Capps also owns a business with the suffix.

Mrs Capps, who founded Kinton & Daughter Funeral Services Ltd aged 19 with her father, Nigel Kinton, said: “To see the ‘and daughter’ name makes me so happy and so proud.

“Having a woman’s touch at such a sensitive time is really compelling and empowering for families.”

The now 33-year-old runs the funeral parlour alongside her mother, Darlene Kinton, and husband, Josh Capps, after being inspired by a call-out to a care home with her father, who worked in the industry.

“The discrimination that I have faced because of my gender has only made me more determined; it gives me more fire, more want, more drive to give people the opportunity to say goodbye to their loved ones exactly how they want to do it,” she told The Telegraph.

She said people often preferred to have a female funeral conductor over a male conductor, but now they had that option.

Corin Camenisch, SumUp’s global marketing lead, said: “A business name is one of the first decisions an entrepreneur makes, but for family businesses it carries something deeper, a statement of who built it, who will inherit it and who the future belongs to.

“For generations, that statement rarely included a daughter’s name. The tide is beginning to turn.

“It is exciting, albeit overdue, to see more business owners choosing to reflect their daughters in the future of their companies.”

data.title: Royal Mail’s debt-fuelled foreign takeover is failing to deliver
data.description:
The Royal Mail is never slow to come up with excuses. If only the same could be said of delivery times, Britain might have the world’s best postal system.

In response to another deluge of complaints, last month, Royal Mail blamed “adverse weather, including storms Goretti, Ingrid and Chandra, alongside higher than usual sick absence”.

Still, it makes a change from blaming Covid. Industry regulator Ofcom told the company in late 2022 it couldn’t continue to blame the pandemic for its shortcomings.

“Social distancing measures are no longer in place, absence levels are likely to be much less unpredictable and parcel volumes have largely returned to pre-pandemic trends,” Ofcom said.

The £3.6bn debt-fuelled takeover of the Royal Mail by secretive Czech billionaire Daniel Kretinsky was supposed to herald a break with the chronic failings of the past.

Despite widespread warnings about the risk of allowing a 500-year-old critical institution to fall to a debt-laden bid by a businessman based hundreds of miles away in Prague, the Labour Government breezily waved through the deal in late 2024.

The verdict of Jonathan Reynolds, then business secretary, was unequivocal. Kretinsky’s swoop would be “a good deal for the UK, a good deal for the people who work for Royal Mail and a good deal for customers”, Reynolds boldly predicted.

“We have negotiated something which secures the long-term future of Royal Mail and gives it the fresh start that we need,” he said.

Yet, just nine months after Royal Mail’s independence was brought to an end, the mood in Westminster has swung from optimism to anger and anxiety as Kretinsky’s EP investment arm faces growing criticism for failing to deliver.

Concern is so great that Kretinsky has been summoned to appear before MPs on the Commons business and trade select committee. For someone whose penchant for secrecy has earned him the nickname the “Czech Sphinx”, the full scrutiny of Westminster may come as a shock.

MPs are demanding answers. Liam Byrne, the chairman of the committee, wrote to Alistair Cochrane, Royal Mail’s chief executive, in February to express “significant concerns about the quality of postal service being provided by Royal Mail”.

The correspondence came just weeks after Blair McDougall, the business minister responsible for postal services, told the Commons that Royal Mail had been made aware “the way they are operating is not good enough and that they are on notice”.

“I was given an absolute commitment that it will work to deliver the best possible service to customers, while accepting that there have been service challenges,” McDougall added, in response to questions from multiple MPs on behalf of frustrated constituents.

Yet his reassurances failed to placate Byrne. Less than a fortnight later, amid reports that Royal Mail letters were sitting undelivered “for weeks” because staff had been prioritising more lucrative parcels, Byrne fired off his letter.

Copying in both McDougall, and Melanie Dawes, the boss of Ofcom, Byrne told Cochrane he had been made aware of more than 100 postcodes across the UK that were at risk of service disruption because of “local issues such as high levels of sick absence, resourcing, or other local factors”, and as a result, the committee was launching an immediate investigation.

Byrne, who served as Treasury minister in Gordon Brown’s government, outlined a long list of questions, including: “Can you categorically assure the committee that Royal Mail does not operate a general policy of prioritising parcel deliveries over letters?”

Royal Mail bosses were given a fortnight to respond.

Byrne also castigated the company for its performance. Only 77pc of first-class mail and 92.5pc of second-class mail were delivered on time last year – “well short of the 93pc and 98.5pc targets respectively that applied to such deliveries”, he said.

The MP reminded Cochrane that it was only in October last year that the regulator had fined Royal Mail £21m for failing to meet its delivery targets.

“Millions of important letters are arriving late, and people aren’t getting what they pay for when they buy a stamp,” Ofcom said at the time.

“These persistent failures are unacceptable, and customers expect and deserve better. Royal Mail must rebuild consumers’ confidence as a matter of urgency.”

In many ways, such concerns are nothing new. As Byrne pointed out, the watchdog’s latest rebuke was “the third time Ofcom had found the company in breach of its regulatory obligations in as many years”, with fines during the period totalling £36m.

Yet Kretinsky pledged things would be different. Following government approval, EP Group said its mission was “to make Royal Mail a successful modern postal operator with high-quality service and products for its customers”.

Kretinsky, meanwhile, pledged to “put employees and customers at the heart of everything”. He also agreed to no redundancies and to honour pre-agreed pay settlements for Royal Mail’s 90,000-strong workforce of postal workers.

‘A failing service’

Those commitments, along with a growing desire to see the back of the incumbent regime, even convinced the notoriously pugnacious Communication Workers’ Union (CWU) to back the deal.

Dave Ward, the general secretary of the CWU, said the takeover was the “best opportunity” to save the Royal Mail, having previously described the status quo as “not tenable”.

Elsewhere, Ofcom laid out the equivalent of the red carpet, tearing up the Universal Service Obligation (USO) so that second-class letters are delivered every other weekday at the same time as scrapping Saturday services altogether.

The move would “give it the best chance of survival”, and help improve reliability, the regulator said.

The company insists modernisation is crucial to profitability in a world where Amazon and other delivery firms are not bound by the same constraints.

Critics say the delivery problems are particularly hard to accept given another round of stamp price rises.

Royal Mail has announced it will increase the price of a first-class stamp by 10p to £1.80 next month. It’s the seventh price rise in four years and a near doubling from 95p in 2022.

Citizens Advice said customers are being forced “without clear justification … to dig deeper into their pockets for a failing service”.

After a brief détente, the Royal Mail and the CWU are at loggerheads once again.

The company argues that service improvements can only occur through implementation of the USO reforms Ofcom has approved – but to do this, its entire operations, including longstanding delivery routes, must be overhauled and that requires CWU sign-off.

Yet despite a commitment from the union to reach an agreement on a new “detailed deployment plan” by the middle of last July, the two sides are still in negotiations.

Ward, of the CWU, says Royal Mail’s problems stem from the introduction of “lower wages, longer hours and poorer terms and conditions” for postal workers in 2022. The changes had created “a recruitment crisis” – “27,000 new entrants have left” since they were imposed, he told the BBC last week.

The high turnover is partly the result of new postal workers underestimating the physicality of walking an average of nearly seven miles a day, Royal Mail insiders argue.

The threat of another record fine looms large in negotiations. Ofcom officials are understood to be frustrated about the delays to implementation of the USO reforms, and the regulator has warned that more penalties could follow unless Royal Mail produces “a credible improvement plan” soon.

In the meantime, the company points to the rollout of parcel lockers and upgrades to postboxes so they can accept larger parcels as evidence of fresh investment. Yet in his response to the committee, Cochrane rejected claims that parcels were being prioritised other than in exceptional circumstances.

“We do not operate a general policy of prioritising parcel deliveries over letters,” he said, but admitted that “in exceptionally busy periods like Christmas, or when we are faced with short-term resourcing issues, it may be logistically necessary to clear parcels first”.

Regardless of who is at fault, it’s a fact that Royal Mail’s delivery rates remain well below targets, with 219 million letters expected to arrive late this year, leaving Cochrane and Kretinsky with some explaining to do when they appear before the committee.

As well as questions about poor service, the pair may find themselves questioned about the financial constraints of Kretinsky’s debt-financed takeover, which lumbered the Royal Mail with an additional £3bn of high-interest borrowings.

Liam Byrne, the committee’s chairman, said: “This investigation is exposing just how bad Royal Mail has become.”

A Royal Mail spokesman said: “We recognise there is more to do to deliver a consistently high level of service everywhere. Attending the business and trade committee will give us the opportunity to discuss the work we are doing to transform Royal Mail.”

EP Group declined to comment.

data.title: Iran’s attack on Qatar gas citadel blows up Britain’s energy security
data.description:
Soaring prices, fuel shortages and possibly even blackouts: Iran’s latest attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure suggest the impacts of the conflict will be with us for many months to come.

Missiles launched from Iran rained down on the Ras Laffan plant in Qatar overnight, a facility so large that it produces around a fifth of the global supply of liquefied natural gas (LNG).

Many see the attack as a turning point in the war, signalling it will no longer be over in weeks, as Donald Trump promised, but will instead take many months.

It also marks a significant escalation in the conduct of the conflict: until now, Iran has avoided attacking energy infrastructure directly.

Qatar was the biggest supplier of LNG to the UK until 2022, accounting for 40pc of all imports at its peak in 2011. LNG is a key fuel for the UK, which still relies on gas for 35pc of its energy.

Imports have declined dramatically in recent years as the UK has turned to alternative supply from the US, with Qatari LNG now only accounting for around 2pc of imports.

Yet that does not mean Britain will be insulated from the crisis. Gas is a commodity traded globally and a shock in one geography affects the whole market.

European gas prices soared from €55 per megawatt-hour (MWh) to €72/MWh when that market opened on Thursday, a huge jump even before the scale of the damage was known. Oil has surged too – up around 8pc to $116 a barrel. Just a few weeks ago it was trading at $65.

Crisis looms

Qatar Energy, the owner of the Ras Laffan site, said the plant has suffered “extensive” damage, suggesting it could be disabled for months.

Analysts say the impact could be disastrous. Natasha Fielding, who leads on gas and LNG for Argus Media, a commodities specialist, says long-term damage could see prices hit 2022 energy crisis levels.

“If there is long-term damage to the LNG facility – which we still do not know – then it would not be surprising to see prices at the European [benchmark] push significantly higher,” she says.

“They could get closer to the eye-watering heights in the 2022 energy crisis.”

Seb Kennedy, founder of Energy Flux, a gas and LNG analytics platform, takes an even more pessimistic view.

“Rapid escalatory attacks and destruction of critical energy infrastructure are on a scale that defies historical comparison,” he says.

“This spiralling conflict is sowing the seeds of an energy crisis that will dwarf the events of 2021-22.”

What would that mean for the UK? In 2022, the typical annual household energy bill soared from £1,277 to £3,549 in just a few months.

Households never paid that price because the government stepped in to cap average bills at £2,500, but the cost of that was paid through taxes – an impact that has helped undermine the UK’s economy ever since.

We could be heading that way again. In fact, the current crisis means we could see shortages and even blackouts, according to Ashley Kelty of Panmure Liberum investment bank.

“There is a huge risk of shortages – north-west Europe could run out of gas as storage is very limited and competition from Asia means LNG cargoes will be diverted at the least. Load-shedding [where heavy industry is asked to shut down] and even blackouts in the summer no longer look fanciful.”

All forms of energy are set to rise sharply in price – power, gas, petrol, diesel and jet fuel – and the effect on the UK is set to be profound.

Greg Newman, founder and chief executive of Onyx Capital, a major London-based trader in oil and energy derivatives, predicts forecourt prices will hit £2 a litre for diesel as early as next month.

“What is concerning for the UK is that European oil prices, which were lagging behind the Middle East and Asia, are now aggressively catching up – and there is a long way to go for prices just to align with the rest of the world,” he says.

The price of diesel has already risen by more than 20p a litre in the UK to 162.66p since the conflict began, according to the RAC, while petrol has risen by almost 10p a litre to 142.62p.

“We are very confident the diesel price will surpass £2 a litre in the next month,” says Mr Newman.

Iran triggers chaos

What is Iran’s strategy? And why is it attacking facilities owned by neighbours who have taken no part in the US-Israeli strikes?

It’s worth remembering that Iran did not start this war – and nor did it start the attacks on energy infrastructure.

In the first week of Operation Epic Fury, the US and Israel focused on air defences, missile silos and leadership.

Energy infrastructure was largely spared to avoid a global energy crisis, a clear risk right from the start.

All that changed on March 7 when the Israeli Air Force struck multiple fuel storage facilities and oil depots in Tehran’s suburbs.

This week Israel shifted its attacks to target facilities that produce oil and gas, rather than just storing it.

That included South Pars, the world’s largest natural gas field, and the Assaluyeh refinery complex and gas processing plants. That knocked out 20pc of Iran’s gas processing capacity and caused widespread blackouts across Tehran and central Iran. Some 90pc of Iran’s electricity comes from gas-powered plants.

With Israel and the US well protected against retaliation, Iran’s best and perhaps only hope of putting pressure on its enemies lies in disrupting global energy supplies.

It is targeting facilities across the region but there are five key sites it really wants to damage, according to Aditya Saraswat, senior vice-president at global energy analysts Rystad Energy.

Qatar’s Ras Laffan LNG plant is top of the list given its importance in supplying gas globally. Iran’s strike has knocked out the LNG “trains”, or processing lines, supplying Europe, Japan, South Korea and China under long-term contracts.

Similarly, Saudi Arabia’s advanced Samref refinery has faced a series of attacks, the latest on Thursday, so far with little apparent damage. It processes 402,000 barrels of oil a day into petrol, diesel, jet fuel and many more products.

“This is truly the worst case scenario for energy and fuel markets the world over. As usual, the poorest in society stand first in line to bear the brunt of the economic impacts,” says Mr Kennedy.

“Export controls and rationing are already under way in numerous Asian economies. But the scale of supply loss exceeds the ability of governments to manage demand destruction in an orderly fashion.”

The Middle East produces far more than just gas and oil, however. Iran is also targeting Saudi Arabia’s Al Jubail isocyanate production facility, which makes polyurethanes needed for a wide range of plastics used in industries ranging from construction to automotive.

Also under fire is Qatar’s Mesaieed Petrochemical Complex, 25 miles south of Doha, which turns gas and oil into vital chemicals such as polyethylene, the basis for thousands of other plastic products.

And Al Hosn sulphur plant in the United Arab Emirates is a target too. The plant lies above the Shah gas field whose “sour” gas is a key source of sulphur used in the global production of fertilisers and sulphuric acid – perhaps the world’s most widely used chemical.

Mr Saraswat says: “The breadth of what is at risk here in fuels, chemicals, LNG and fertiliser inputs is what makes this moment qualitatively different from previous episodes of Gulf tension.”

Mr Kennedy says: “Even if military warfare ends immediately, the energy situation is going to get a lot worse before it gets better.”

The mounting pressure on energy is increasing pressure on the UK Government to step in and help struggling households and businesses. Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, has said “nothing is off the table”. But with public finances still bearing the scars of the last round of support in 2022, there is a practical limit to just how far ministers can go.

Whatever happens, the cost of energy is set to be a key issue in the upcoming local and regional elections, including the Scottish and Welsh parliamentary elections, seen as vital in deciding whether Sir Keir Starmer still has a future as PM.

Our energy security has been shattered. And while it is not his fault, it may be Sir Keir who pays the price.

data.title: Sky News to cut ties with UAE channel
data.description:
Sky is preparing to pull the plug on its controversial news joint venture with the United Arab Emirates (UAE) after the channel was accused of propaganda and genocide denial.

The British satellite broadcaster has effectively given notice that it will withdraw Sky News Arabia’s licence to use its brand next year, The Telegraph can reveal.

It is understood that Sky executives delivered the news to the UAE’s state media business, IMI, late last year and have completed the necessary legal work for the licence to lapse.

Communications between the two sides remain open and an agreement to salvage the partnership could yet be reached. 

Sky declined to comment. IMI initially declined to comment but, after this story was published, a spokesman said it was “incorrect” to say any decisions had been taken on the future of the relationship.

“Any suggestion that decisions have been taken on the future of this partnership is incorrect,” they said.

“Discussions are actively under way, and both sides remain fully and positively engaged in that process.”

The spokesman said the discussions were commercially focused, and so confidential, but not tied to editorial matters.

The upcoming split follows Sky News Arabia being accused of whitewashing atrocities carried out by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), a Sudanese paramilitary group backed by the UAE, according to Western intelligence agencies.

Sheikh Mansour, the Manchester City owner who also funds IMI, has allegedly played a key role controlling the UAE’s activities in Sudan. The UAE has denied culpability for atrocities committed by the RSF.

In 2024, the Gulf state lashed out at the UK via the former Conservative minister Nadhim Zahawi for “standing by while the Sudanese defame them” at the UN.

IMI had separately in 2023 attempted to take control of The Telegraph in a partnership with RedBird Capital Partners, a US private equity firm. The deal was blocked following an outcry over press freedom.

After extended wrangling, the pair have agreed to sell the newspaper on to the German publisher Axel Springer.

The decision by Sky to break ties with IMI will bring to an end a 50-50 partnership first agreed in 2010.

At the time, Sky was under the control of News Corp and Rupert Murdoch, who was seeking to build lucrative alliances with the UAE and planned to licence his entertainment brands to 20th Century Fox World, a theme park in Dubai.

However, the project was cancelled in 2018 before construction began.

Sky has been owned by the US cable giant Comcast since 2018. Its withdrawal from the UAE partnership will come after it also ended the licence held by News Corp to use the Sky News brand in Australia.

There, the tone of the channel is closer to Mr Murdoch’s partisan US juggernaut Fox News than to Sky News in the UK, which operates under strict impartiality regulations.

Sky News Arabia initially set out to bring the highest international standards to coverage of the Middle East, but UK-based former Sky executives told The Telegraph in November that the channel had become a mouthpiece for the UAE’s rulers.

One said an editorial board meant to monitor coverage had “no real power” as the channel was “owned by (UAE vice-president) Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed”.

Sky News Arabia generated international controversy last year in its reporting on the RSF’s capture of El Fasher, the capital of Darfur state.

Satellite imagery documented a massacre of thousands of civilians, with so many shot in the streets that piles of blood and bodies were visible from space.

In February, a UN fact-finding mission reported that an intent to commit genocide was the “only reasonable inference” from the pattern of rape, murder and ethnic cleansing.

But Sky News Arabia sent a reporter to the scene who is married to a senior official in the RSF’s parallel government.

Tsabih Mubarak Khatir was filmed hugging a female RSF commander who had urged fighters to rape Darfuri women, telling her “we are with you”.

She then filed reports claiming there was no evidence of the atrocities documented by satellite imagery or media reports gathered from those who managed to flee.

On its news website, Sky News Arabia ran articles suggesting that the satellite imagery was fake news.

The UAE is widely accused of backing the RSF in Sudan’s civil war as part of an effort to gain access to the country’s valuable ports, gold reserves and farmland.

IMI defended Sky News Arabia’s coverage in a statement to The Telegraph, saying accusations of bias were “outrageous and unfounded”.

A spokesman also said it was “categorical fake news” that the board of editorial standards had ceased to function effectively.

UK-based Sky News did not respond to detailed requests for comment on the reporting of its UAE-based sister channel.

data.title: Spandex maker stretched past breaking point as Lycra goes bankrupt
data.description:
As anyone who has owned a pair of leggings will know, there’s a limit to how far they’ll stretch. Now, Lycra has found the same holds true of its finances.

Spandex maker Lycra has filed for bankruptcy amid growing debt pressures and competition from overseas rivals.

The Lycra Company produces the namesake elastic fibre used by scores of brands in products ranging from cycling shorts and women’s yoga leggings. The company has launched a voluntary restructuring process to cut borrowings of $1.2bn (£890m), with lenders taking control of the business for the second time in four years. 

Developed in the 1950s by scientists at DuPont Textiles in the United States, the cutting-edge elastic fibre quickly became a staple for women’s underwear and fitness clothing.

After DuPont launched its Lycra brand, it was popoularised by celebrities such as Audrey Hepburn, Joan Collins and Ann-Margret, who posed in skin-tight products made from Lycra in advertisements and on magazine covers.

Lycra was further pushed into the cultural zeitgeist by Jane Fonda and the aerobics trend in the 1980s, making the clothing so popular that DuPont struggled to meet demand.

While household names like Spanx, which uses Lycra in its shapewear and other products, have been overtaken by new brands, the fitness category is booming, which should boost the company. 

This includes Ms Kardashian’s company, Skims. Launched in 2019, Skims now has 18 stores in the US alone and generates over $1bn in sales.

It disrupted the shapewear industry by marketing Gen-Z-friendly products designed to be worn by a variety of body types.

The success of Ms Kardashian’s brand, which makes products ranging from sleepwear to sportswear, quickly took market share from Spanx, whose more dowdy image is often associated with figures like Bridget Jones.

Other popular new fitness brands include Adanola, Alo Yoga and Tala.

While the fitness and shapewear industry is booming, Lycra has suffered from increasing competition from cut price manufacturers in places like China.

In its bankruptcy filing, the company said: “The industry has also experienced significant capacity expansions by competitors, altering the competitive dynamics and leading to decreased utilization rates across the company’s manufacturing facilities.

“The company has faced intensifying competition from low-cost manufacturers, particularly in Asia, which has placed downward pressure on pricing and eroded market share in certain segments. Generic spandex prices have fallen to near cash-cost levels, compressing margins across the industry.”

In 2019, Koch Industries – which had taken control of DuPont in 2004 – sold The Lycra Company to Chinese textiles and clothing company Shandong Ruyi, where it soon ran into trouble with creditors.

The company has once again been hammered by a series of headwinds, including reduced demand for sportswear and supply chain challenges.

By the end of last year, production rates across Lycra’s eight production facilities had fallen to around 60pc, according to Bloomberg News, while its earnings before tax were projected to slump to $44m this year, from $132m in 2024.

Lycra’s bankruptcy will likely cause alarm among members of Britain’s cycling community who are known for their love of skin-tight spandex outfits.

However, the company said the manufacturing facilities will not be affected.

“We will continue serving our customers, supporting our partners, and providing the high-quality products on which they rely,” said Gary Smith, Lycra’s chief executive.

data.title: British aid worker debanked while on humanitarian mission
data.description:
Have you been debanked? Tell us your story by emailing money@telegraph.co.uk*

Jonathan Brass works in dangerous places. During his decades-long career as an aid worker, he’s helped manage teams and coordinate responses to some of the worst crises countries can face in places such as Ukraine, Turkey and Honduras.

The 49-year-old father-of-two is currently working in Myanmar, helping those devastated by the 7.7-magnitude earthquake that hit the country last March. Thousands of people died.

As operations manager for an international aid organisation, running a team on the ground is no small feat. But earlier this year, while Brass was waiting in Malaysia for his visa, his UK bank accounts with Halifax and Lloyds were suddenly closed.

“I logged into my Halifax account to see what my situation was,” he says. “I do have a credit card which needs paying off but there was no money in the account.

“I went to my Lloyds account and saw the same thing. I presumed it was a glitch because these things sometimes do happen.”

When Brass contacted the banks, they told him that he had been debanked. Lloyds Banking Group owns Halifax, and so both accounts were closed.

When he asked why, they said they could not tell him but that he should have been notified by letter that his account was being closed.

Brass has a house in East Sussex – his base in Britain – but because he has been working abroad, he didn’t receive the letters, and the banks did not contact him by other means. This also meant that he hasn’t been able to collect the cheques sent with his closing balances.

“I never received the letters. I am sure they are at my home,” he says.

Brass also couldn’t call the bank from Myanmar to ask more questions about what had happened.

While there was not a huge sum of money in his closed accounts, he was worried that his credit card being cut off could paralyse him in an emergency. He claims he has previously told his banks about his job as an aid worker.

“[The credit card has] been my only lifeline in some countries,” Brass says. “Particularly somewhere like Myanmar where there are sanctions, there are restrictions on banking.

“Maybe something erupts and you need to get out of the country quickly or you need to have access to some kind of financing.”

Debanking has become endemic in Britain. An estimated 453,230 accounts were shut down in 2024-25, according to figures released to The Telegraph under Freedom of Information rules.

This was a more than tenfold increase on the 45,091 accounts closed in 2016-17, and an 11pc jump on the 408,000 accounts closed in the 2023-24 tax year.

Banks often refuse to share their reasoning about account closures on the basis that sharing why lenders shut accounts could help money launderers and criminals better evade their investigations.

But after Nigel Farage was debanked by Coutts in 2023, the Reform leader adopted the banking practice as a campaigning issue. He has previously blamed European legislation for the increasing numbers of people being debanked. He claimed it “makes it cheaper for banks to close accounts over unusual transactions”.

From April 28, stricter rules introduced by the Government will mean banks have to tell their customers further in advance about closures and provide better explanations. Customers will be given 90 days’ notice rather than the current two months.

But these requirements will only apply to newly opened accounts – meaning that Brass would not have been owed any more information.

“It’s frustrating. You think you deserve some customer service, some loyalty, for being with the bank for so long,” Brass says. He spent decades as a customer and says his father had opened his first account for him when he was a child.

“There’s an algorithm somewhere which doesn’t quite understand why aid workers are in all these funny countries. It’s put two and two together to make six and felt I deserved cancelling.”

Brass is now worried that the business accounts for his British-based wedding rings company will also be targeted.

Brass says: “I do live slightly in fear that they will close my business account and then that will be in a bit of trouble.”

A spokesman for Lloyds said: “We understand how important it is for people to manage their money safely and reliably.

“Accounts are only closed for a small number of reasons. In this case, we requested information from Brass several times prior to closing the account, including by email and letters to his registered address, but we didn’t receive a reply.”

After The Telegraph contacted the bank on Brass’s behalf, Lloyds contacted Brass directly by email. He will be able to keep his credit cards and has been invited to open a new account.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Bankers may be pariahs, but they are Britain’s great strength
data.description:
Financial services have not had a good write up since the global financial crisis when the UK government was forced to commit nearly £1tn to save the economy from ruin. Even though the eventual cost was much lower, memories of bankers paying themselves generous bonuses throughout continue to rankle with members of the public. Stepping in to support banks is never easy for a Chancellor, as I found during the rescue of Silicon Valley Bank’s London subsidiary in 2023.

But the world has changed dramatically since 2008. Whether the Government wants to or not, it is going to have to spend more on defence – as events in Iran have shown. Borrowing costs may well soar. So with public finances stretched to breaking point, it’s time to recognise more explicitly the extent to which financial services are a major contributor to our economy.

Together with insurance services, they account for around 8 per cent of our output and generate over £70bn in tax receipts. If you add on professional services, often connected because of the legal and consultancy services required in the City, the figure jumps to £110bn. That’s around a tenth of all tax receipts and more than half the cost of running the NHS.

As Chancellor I recognised how strategic this sector is for the UK. I pushed through the Edinburgh and Mansion House reforms to improve its competitiveness and boost the liquidity of UK capital markets. Listing rules in London are now largely competitive with Nasdaq, something vital for our technology sector, now the third largest in the world after the US and China.

But there is much more to do, so I have now become the new chair of the Financial Markets and Services All Party Parliamentary Group.

Put simply, at a time of slow growth, we don’t have many good options. So it should be a no-brainer to back one of our most promising sectors. Many predicted Brexit would fatally damage the City but in fact it has demonstrated its resilience. Today half of the world’s over-the-counter interest rate derivatives are traded in the UK, as is one third of all foreign currency exchange. Despite intense competition, London remains Europe’s largest insurance market. Britain is still the world’s largest net exporter of financial services and the second largest exporter of services overall.

So we continue to punch above our weight. But competition is hotting up. Post-Brexit France has been trying hard to persuade companies to move their European headquarters to Paris. High net worth individuals have been moving to Milan and – until recently – Dubai.

So far the damage has been relatively limited but the risk to London’s pre-eminence – and to vital tax revenue for the Treasury – is growing. So it is imperative that the Government accepts recommendations of the recent City UK report which includes taking the lead in financial technologies such as AI and tokenised assets, making it easier to do business by simplifying laws, regulations and taxes, being more strategic internationally to secure the UK’s financial services position through partnerships and promoting more investment from both institutional and retail investors.

To create a new culture around retail and institutional investing will mean changing the way we approach risk. That’s why we need to press on with consolidation of the UK pension fund industry, as former Lord Mayor Sir Nicholas Lyons and I argued when we introduced the Mansion House Compact.

The next step is to follow reforms pioneered in Australia which mean that instead of multiple pension pots, savers have one pension pot that follows them from job to job. The result is that ordinary Australians have become retail investors through their pension pots. Fierce competition between superannuation funds to attract them has led to returns, on average, 1.6 per cent higher than in the UK. If that was sustained over 40 years of saving, it would mean pension pots roughly double the size of ours. It also means more investment in private infrastructure projects and high growth businesses, whether pre or post IPO.

The Government has said it wants to continue with the Edinburgh and Mansion House reforms and, in fairness, has identified Financial Services as one of the eight industrial sectors with the greatest growth potential. I look forward to working closely with City Minister Lucy Rigby.

But we will need more urgency if we are to grasp the prize. City UK says its recommendations could increase tax receipts by £22bn as well as create jobs and growth. At a time when the rest of the economy appears stuck in a low-growth trap, that is not to be sniffed at.

Rt Hon Sir Jeremy Hunt MP has served as Chancellor of the Exchequer

data.title: Heritage pottery brand on brink of collapse with 500 jobs at risk
data.description:
A 200-year-old pottery business is on the verge of collapse, putting up to 500 jobs at risk.

Denby, which has been manufacturing at the same site in Derby since it was founded in 1809, announced on Wednesday that it had filed a notice to appoint administrators.

The company said in its last available accounts that it had faced “another challenging year” of weak consumer confidence and stubborn inflation.

Denby has been seeking investment to secure its future but has so far been unable to secure a backer, leaving it with no option but to prepare for administration as a precaution.

The business evolved from small workshops in the 19th century to a sprawling manufacturing site in the heart of Derbyshire, where most of the company’s 500 employees are based.

The clay used in Denby’s products has been dug from the original seam first discovered more than 200 years ago.

During the Second World War effort, Denby produced “utility” brownwares, including Naafi teapots, rum bottles, insulators and battery jars.

It led the “oven-to-tableware” revolution of the 1950s, which later became synonymous with aspirational middle-class British homes.

Denby was acquired by furniture conglomerate Coloroll in 1987 and was floated on the London Stock Exchange, but the business was later taken private following a management buyout.

The company had come under the ownership of turnaround experts Hilco Capital by 2009.

In more recent years, Denby has found success in Asia and has an office in Gangnam, South Korea.

The brand also enjoyed a brief moment of cult success after its products appeared in Netflix’s Squid Game. Denby stoneware has also regularly appeared on the Great British Bake-Off and EastEnders.

Hayley Baddiley, the company’s marketing director, said the durability of Denby products caused issues on the set of Emmerdale.

“It was part of a big, dramatic scene where characters were having a row, and one of our mugs was to be thrown, crashing to the floor, but they had to do it seven times because they couldn’t get it to break,” she told Innovate magazine.

The threat of administration comes after decades of wavering fortunes for Denby, which at times was viewed as unfashionable.

Throughout the 2010s, Denby engineered a quiet revival, leaning into sustainability and reissuing mid-century patterns.

But Denby’s post-tax losses widened from £3.1m to £5.56m in the year to December 2024, according to the company’s latest accounts, despite the firm reducing investment and cutting costs.

The group said it would continue to seek a potential investor for either the entire business or individual brands. It added that supporting employees, customers and suppliers was a priority.

Denby was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain’s biggest car park operator goes bust
data.description:
Britain’s biggest car park operator has collapsed into administration, blaming a downturn in commuting and inner city shopping since the pandemic.

National Car Parks (NCP), which runs 340 car parks across the UK, said 682 jobs were at risk after PwC was called in to try and save the business.

It will keep sites open while the administrators assess its options for the future, including a potential sale or site closures.

NCP collapsed after struggling with rent rises linked to inflation and “elevated” energy costs, according to its Japanese owner.

The 95-year-old business also blamed Covid-19 for a downturn in demand for parking in city centres and commuter hotspots.

PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Its collapse follows criticism of private parking companies like NCP for issuing parking tickets. Private parking operators that issued a record 15.9 million tickets last year, according to an analysis of government data.

The industry has been accused of using confusing signs to catch drivers out and aggressive debt-collection to collect fines from customers.

Business grew in post-war boom

One of Britain’s best known corporate names, NCP grew out of the bomb craters of post-war London. It was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before being merged with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

Central Car Parks was itself founded by ex-servicemen Sir Ronald Hobson and Sir Donald Gosling in 1948. The pair spent £200 on turning a bomb site in Holborn, central London, into a car park.

After acquiring NCP, the entrepreneurs went on to rapidly expand their business, leveraging from the post-war boom in privately owned cars.

Eventually they owned more than 500 town-centre car parks – some 70pc of the parking market in the UK – as well as petrol stations, the Green Flag breakdown service and airport buses.

Hobson and Gosling’s families made £580m when they sold NCP to US company Cendant Corporation in 1998. The company changed hands several times after that before ending up under the ownership of Japan’s Park24.

PwC said NCP faced a cash crunch this month as “significant rent payments” fell due. Administrators said it had become “increasingly difficult to secure the necessary funding”. NCP lost £5.7m last year, following a £10.1m loss the previous year.

‘Challenging trading environment’

Zeif Hussain, of PwC, said: “NCP has faced a challenging trading environment over several years, with changing consumer behaviours impacting volumes, and a high fixed cost-base leading to trading losses.

“Our priority on appointment is to ensure continuity of service while we undertake a detailed review of the business.

“All sites are open, staff remain in post, and trading continues as normal. We will be engaging with landlords, employees and other stakeholders as we explore all options, including the potential sale of all or part of the business, to secure the best possible outcome for creditors.”

NCP’s collapse comes after Park24 last year admitted it had over-counted the number of parking spaces offered by the UK business. Park24 said at the time that the incident would not affect its earnings for its 2025 financial year.

Gosling, who died in 2019, was knighted in 1976 and appointed as Knight Commander of the Royal Victoria Order in 2004. In 2012, Elizabeth II appointed him Vice Admiral of the UK, a ceremonial appointment reflecting his patronage of various naval charities.

An ardent royalist, Gosling called for entrepreneurs to club together to fund a replacement Royal Yacht when Britannia was decommissioned in 1997, offering £5m of his own money.

data.title: Water boss accused of potential conflict of interest over lobbying ties
data.description:
The chairman of a water quango that sticks up for consumers has been accused of a potential conflict of interest over his role at a lobbying firm that works with utilities.

Rob Wilson, who runs the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), is a senior adviser at Political Insight UK, The Telegraph can reveal.

Mr Wilson, a former Conservative minister under David Cameron, is one of four senior advisers at the firm and a shareholder in the business.

Founded in 2020, Political Insight UK claims on its website to help clients with “government relations” by advising them on “policy development and regulatory issues”.

This includes clients across a range of sectors, including transport, housing, retail and public utilities. The website makes no mention of individual water companies, though the term utilities covers the water industry.

His links to the lobbying firm have raised concerns among MPs and campaigners owing to his role at CCW, a Government-approved quango funded by households and businesses.

The CCW brands itself as the voice of billpayers by holding companies, government and regulators to account.

Alistair Carmichael, chairman of the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee, said: “Public confidence in the water industry is already at an all-time low.

“A body like the CCW has to be seen to be the consumers’ voice and nothing else. This is not a moment where anyone can be seen to be riding two horses.”

Peter Hammond, a retired professor turned campaigner, also raised concerns over a conflict of interest if Mr Wilson’s firm advised water companies.

The latest revelations will heighten pressure on Mr Wilson after a cache of receipts revealed how he twice claimed discounts on train tickets last year by using a railcard for students.

On one occasion, he was caught by a conductor and asked to pay a £16.40 fare correction, which he later expensed.

The CCW said Mr Wilson had clicked the 16-25 railcard by accident and made the mistake in “good faith”.

Mr Wilson previously drew scrutiny during his time as a Tory MP for claiming 9p for a 350-yard car journey.

As well as serving as an adviser to Political Insight UK, the former minister is also a director for Transport Focus, a consumer action group for rail passengers.

In recent years, Transport Focus has worked in tandem with government officials to combat fare evasion.

The CCW says on its website that Mr Wilson, who was appointed chairman by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) last year, has a “number of shareholdings declared”.

However, the group said that “the list does not include any water companies”.

‘Radical transparency’

A CCW spokesman said the conflict of interest claims were a matter for Defra.

Defra asks public appointees to disclose any potential conflicts of interest to the department and puts mitigation measures in place if necessary.

Speaking last year, Mr Wilson called for “radical transparency” and greater accountability to rebuild trust in the water sector.

He also spoke to water companies about how to address whether their actions are acceptable at a conference.

“If this were explained on the front page of tomorrow’s newspaper, would the public see it as fair, responsible and in their interest?” he said, addressing attendees in his capacity as CCW chairman.

“If the answer is yes, you are rebuilding confidence. If the answer is no, you are eroding it.”

Scrutiny of Mr Wilson’s lobbying ties comes at an awkward time for the CCW.

The group has so far failed to publish financial accounts as it battles with the National Audit Office (NAO) amid a dispute over its expenditure.

Analysis of the CCW’s board minutes from last year also highlights how the group is in a separate spat with HMRC.

A CCW spokesman said this matter concerned a decision not to include VAT on its sales invoices.

“After extensive communication with HMRC, we have now become VAT registered to allow us to correct the issue, which we are in the process of doing,” the spokesman said.

“Having revisited the calculations HMRC provides, we understand any resulting interest and surcharges will be minimal.”

Defra was contacted for comment.

data.title: Seven ways to help your children buy their first home
data.description:
Getting on the property ladder has become increasingly difficult over the years. House price growth has long outstripped wages, while rising rents have inhibited tenants’ ability to save, and stricter lending criteria have compounded the problem.

Almost a third of first-time buyers now rely on financial support to buy a home, according to figures from Barclays. And, for most, this means making an often sizeable withdrawal from the Bank of Mum and Dad.

But it can be tricky for parents to work out the best way to approach this and how much they can realistically afford to give.

Here, Telegraph Money explains the main ways to support first-time buyers, including the pros and cons, so you can decide what’s right for your family.

1. Pay into a Lifetime Isa

If your child is aged 18 or over and buying a home is a future aspiration in a few years, encouraging them to open a Lifetime Isa (Lisa) now and offering to contribute to it is a good step.

Anyone aged 18 to 39 can open these accounts and save up to £4,000 a year to put towards their first home or retirement. The big incentive is that the Government adds a 25pc bonus, capped at £1,000 a year.

Richard Dana, of mortgage and savings platform Tembo, said: “If a parent were to support their child from the age of 18 to 28 with the full annual contribution to a Lisa, that would bolster their child’s deposit by £10,000 in bonuses alone. 

“When including compounding interest gains, this accumulates further into a very meaningful sum.”

This could be a good option for those who don’t have a large lump sum sitting waiting for their child, but who can afford to contribute to their child’s savings each tax year.

Lisa funds can be held as cash or invested in stocks and shares, and all interest growth or investment gains are tax-free.

It’s worth noting the Government’s plan to replace the Lifetime Isa in 2028 with an alternative savings product geared towards first-time buyers. It’s understood that anyone who already has a Lisa will be able to continue saving with it after this time.

2. Give a lump sum as a gift

If your child dreams of buying a home soon and you have the means, giving a lump-sum gift is a straightforward way to boost their deposit. A larger deposit can make it easier to qualify for a mortgage and secure more competitive interest rates.

As it’s a gift, the money doesn’t need to be repaid. But this makes it crucial not to risk your own financial security or retirement plans by giving away more than you can afford.

Giving gifts can also have inheritance tax implications.

Tom Evans, of estate agency Purplebricks, explained: “This type of gift is known as a Potentially Exempt Transfer (PET), where if you die within seven years of granting the loan, some or all of it might fall back into your estate for inheritance tax.”

You may be able to reduce the tax risk by using available gift allowances, and it’s essential to keep accurate records of when the money was given.

Mortgage lenders will usually require written confirmation that the funds were a gift and that there is no need to repay them.

3. Provide an interest-free loan

Instead of gifting the money outright, you could provide an interest-free loan. This gives your child similar benefits to a cash gift, but the money must be repaid, limiting the financial impact on you.

From an inheritance tax perspective, this option means the loan remains part of the parents’ estate.

However, Mr Evans warned: “If you opt for the loan route, you need to be able to prove to the HMRC that it was always intended to be repaid. Otherwise, you risk it being reverted to being a gift, which would trigger PET rules.”

The best way to navigate this is to draw up a formal written agreement that outlines the repayment terms and confirms that the loan is interest-free. Both parties must sign it.

Mortgage lenders may be willing to accept this type of arrangement, provided the loan doesn’t need to be repaid immediately to avoid affordability issues.

Mr Evans said: “In practice, this often means your child will not be saddled with monthly repayments, plus the repayment can be deferred until the property is sold or remortgaged.”

4. Remortgage to release funds

Not everyone is fortunate enough to have readily available funds to give to their children. Remortgaging your own property to release cash can be an attractive choice if you have built up significant equity in your home, particularly if mortgage rates are low.

However, taking on additional borrowing later in life can be risky. Your monthly repayments could increase, putting extra strain on your finances, and lenders may restrict the amount you can borrow as you approach retirement.

Alexandra Loydon, of St James’s Place, said: “If you decide to go down this route, you need to be completely comfortable that you have the means by which to repay the mortgage and are happy to absorb the set-up costs.

“You then need to decide whether you’re lending or gifting the funds, or a combination of the two.”

5. Opt for equity release

Those over 55 could consider equity release instead. This enables you to unlock tax-free cash from your property’s value without moving – but it must be used with caution.

The most popular type of equity release is a lifetime mortgage. It’s designed to last your lifetime or until you move into permanent care.

Lifetime mortgages usually come with flexible payment options. This means you can choose to pay interest each month or not pay any interest and have it “rolled up” and added to the loan. When the property is sold, the loan is repaid in full.

The advantage of equity release is that you can help financially support your child while continuing to live in your home. But there are downsides to consider.

Mr Evans said: “Compounding interest can chip away at the value of an estate, making this an option best suited to families who want to provide immediate support over safeguarding future inheritance.”

Because interest builds over time, equity release can significantly reduce the value of what you leave behind. Releasing equity can also affect your entitlement to means-tested benefits. This makes it essential to seek financial advice first.

6. Downsize your home to free up cash

Selling a larger home and moving to a smaller property can seem like a neat way to free up the funds you require without taking on additional borrowing.

Helen Morrissey, of investment platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “You may feel like the family home is now too big for you and need something a bit smaller. You may believe you can release enough money to get that smaller home while releasing enough funds to give your loved one money towards a deposit.”

However, it’s important to do your sums. Moving can be expensive, especially when you factor in stamp duty, estate agent fees and solicitor fees.

Ms Morrissey added: “You also need to make sure that you can release enough money to meet your own housing needs, as well as those of your family members.”

7. Sign on to the mortgage

If it’s not possible to stump up a large cash gift or loan, release equity or move home, lenders such as NatWest and Skipton offer mortgage products aimed at parents wanting to help their children buy a property.

Often known as Joint Borrower, Sole Proprietor (JBSP) mortgages, they allow up to four people to be named on the mortgage.

Phil Spencer, TV property expert and founder of property advice website Move iQ, said: “The principle is that the parents and their adult children borrow the money together, thus allowing everyone’s income to be taken into account.

“The child will own the property – that’s the ‘sole proprietor’ bit – but because they’re borrowing alongside their parents, the lender will lend significantly more than they would if only the child’s income were included.”

Unlike a traditional joint mortgage, where all parties’ names appear on the deeds, JBSP mortgages keep ownership separate from support. However, all borrowers on the mortgage are liable if repayments are not made, so this type of mortgage still carries risk.

Mr Dana added: “Lenders will also assess the finances of everyone involved, meaning existing debts, mortgage commitments or approaching retirement can limit eligibility.”

If you are considering a JSBP mortgage, it’s best to consider it as a temporary arrangement. This means that once your child can afford the mortgage on their own, the parent should be removed from it.

data.title: ‘Rogue builders ran off with £200k and left me homeless’
data.description:
Have you lost money to rogue builders? Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Steve Higgs never envisioned that six years after buying his forever home in Surrey, he would find himself living in a plasterboard shack and £200,000 out of pocket.

But when the building company he hired to redevelop his £625,000 property claimed it had gone bust and abruptly ceased all communication with him, he was left with nothing but foundations and a few half-finished walls despite handing over £170,000 for the work.

Mr Higgs, 39, had set out to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom property in Horley, after he was advised that it had serious asbestos issues.

Since early 2022, he has been forced to live in a makeshift cabin on the grounds of his three-acre estate as his property became uninhabitable.

He last had contact with the building company responsible in January 2025 when they told him they were experiencing “financial troubles” after his home had been demolished.

Mr Higgs, who was paralysed from the chest down in a motorcycle accident when he was 26, said the ordeal had left him emotionally drained, homeless and facing a big hole in his finances.

He said: “It has played havoc on my mental health. They’ve already wasted four years of my life and £170,000. I can’t sit on my hands in this little box any more. I’ve got no windows. It’s just plasterboard walls. I’m literally just living in a cell of my own making.”

It comes as Britain faces a plague of rogue builders that is costing homeowners and the economy billions of pounds a year.

In the past five years alone, homeowners lost an estimated £14.3bn to cowboy builders, while a fear of being ripped off has cost the economy a further £10bn in lost jobs, according to the Federation of Master Builders (FMB) trade body.

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, said the cost of living crisis had created the “perfect environment” for sham building companies to proliferate.

He said: “There are no barriers to entry so it’s easy to come in and scam people. The rogue builder will come along, undercut a decent builder and then, because people are short of cash, they will go for the cheap option. And it’s only getting worse.”

Mr Higgs paid the building company £113,000 to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom house in Horley, Surrey, after he was advised that issues with asbestos and rotten timber frames made it more cost-effective than his original plan to renovate and extend.

He paid an additional £55,000 for roof truss installation and spent £30,000 on buying materials directly from a roofing supplier.

He said the building company had a strong online presence, including a professional website, Facebook reviews and a range of photos displaying previous work, as well as a branded fleet of vans. Up to four labourers a day would appear on site.

However, when Mr Higgs raised concerns about the quality of bricklaying and incorrect proportions for the new foundations, he was told, in correspondence seen by The Telegraph, that the company had “run into financial troubles”.

Despite numerous calls and emails the company stopped responding to queries about when the remedial work would be carried out.

After a few months, he hired a lawyer to draft a letter before action for £7,000, only to be advised that it could cost up to £120,000 pursuing the claim in a civil court.

He was told that even if he won there was no guarantee he would be able to recoup what he paid out as the company was claiming financial distress.

‘Hopeless’ legal process

Currently all builders carrying out work on private homes are regulated by the Consumer Rights Act 2015. It sets out the standards that consumers can expect when a trader supplies building works, and statutory remedies if those rights are breached.

However, if the company is declared insolvent, options for reclaiming money can be extremely limited and outcomes are typically poor.

Mark Garnier, shadow economic secretary, who has campaigned for more stringent controls on building firms, has previously called the legal process for bringing rogue traders to justice “hopeless”.

If the work was paid for using a credit card, homeowners can claim back up to £30,000 under Section 75. But many rogue traders encourage cash-in-hand payments at a discount to avoid creating a paper trail.

Paula Higgins, at the Homeowners’ Alliance trade body, said “phoenix companies” – whereby the owner of an insolvent business simply rebrands under a different name – was an ongoing problem in the construction sector.

She said: “If you investigate certain directors, they will be setting companies up and closing them down all the time – [the regulation] around it is very weak.”

Despite calls from industry leaders for tighter regulation such as a mandatory national licensing scheme ensuring minimum competency for all builders, the Government has dragged its heels on introducing any new legislation.

Mr Gray said the Government didn’t appear to have any plans to implement controls in the construction sector despite “zero protections” for consumers against rogue builders.

He said: “Anyone can call themselves a builder because they’re not regulated. In certain circumstances you can go through the court, but ultimately if the company folds there’s nothing you can do. You can’t claim anything back. You just have to go to the drawing board.”

To avoid falling prey to rogue builders, Ms Higgins said the most important steps were to get a contract in place, avoid paying for projects upfront and in cash, and to carry out extensive research into the company beyond its own website and social media channels.

She also warned against any company that is available at short notice. “The best builders are the ones you have to wait for,” she said, adding that everyone should look on Companies House to see for how long a business has been operating.

For word of mouth recommendations, ensure their previous work is similar to what you are asking for and check previous projects yourself, she added.

Mr Higgs said he had been quoted £23,000 for remedial work, including widening the existing foundation to the correct size. The rest of the project is expected to cost another £90,000, plus VAT.

“It’s pretty much looking at the same figures again,” he said.

Mr Higgs receives an income from a personal injury trust, but said the financial impact would be long-lasting. “It’s definitely thrown a spanner in the works,” he said. “It’s turned what was meant to be a five-year build plan into a 10-year plan.”

A spokesman for the Department for Business and Trade said: “Rogue builders are a scourge for hard-working families. Anyone who is affected can contact their local authority who have new powers to hand out tougher fines against businesses who rip off consumers.”

data.title: ATMs are quietly vanishing and it’s made another basic banking task impossible
data.description:
Technology is supposed to make life easier, but often it only serves to complicate things. Anyone who has ever grappled with the brave new world of digital banking will understand this all too well.

I had my fill of customer-service-by-coding recently when my 12-year-old daughter’s debit card suddenly stopped working. She discovered the problem while out shopping and had to get her friend to pay for her purchases, refunded by me afterwards (BACS payment, natch – like the late Queen, these kids don’t carry cash).

I called NatWest to request a new Pin, only to be told there was just one way to resolve the issue: I had to visit one of the bank’s ATMs. Really? In the age of online banking, with more than 6,000 bank and building society branches closed in the UK since 2015?

According to figures from December, nearly 19,000 free-to-use ATMs have also disappeared from Britain’s high streets since 2018. The infrastructure of cash has quietly been dismantled, even as banks still insist that certain problems can only be solved by physically standing in front of a machine.

Off we trotted to the nearest branch, several miles away. You used to be able to park outside, but not any more. What was once a five-minute dash is now a half-hour expedition involving parking apps, forgotten passwords and verification codes. But imagine dealing with this sort of problem when you’re 92 and wheelchair-bound.

That was exactly what happened to Telegraph reader John Wyborn, who wrote to me this week, describing his own encounter with modern banking’s particular brand of “computer says no”.

“My wife is now 88 and has recently been in hospital. Neither of us now drive,” he explained, before describing how two bank cards – one from HSBC, the other from Lloyds – suddenly stopped working.

“After some telephone difficulty the HSBC card was reinstated but it still didn’t work,” he wrote. Meanwhile, his wife’s Lloyds card also failed. The message repeatedly claimed the wrong Pin had been entered too many times.

“There is no Lloyds branch here any more and no convenient public transport to the nearest branch,” he added.

After many more phone calls, Mr Wyborn finally managed to verify the Pin had been correct all along. His wife tried the card again – and it still failed.

Eventually it emerged nothing was wrong with the Pin at all; the card had simply been suspended. The only way Lloyds could restore it, they were told, was for one of them to visit an ATM.

“To younger, active people none of this may be a problem,” Mr Wyborn told me. “But it really is not acceptable for people of our age to be treated this way.”

HSBC has resolved its issue; Lloyds has not. The bank offered £25 compensation, which Mr Wyborn notes might just cover the taxi to the ATM.

But that is hardly the point. A system that only works if you are young, mobile and digitally fluent really isn’t progress at all.
