data.title: North Sea giant ready to exploit UK’s biggest oil field this year
data.description:
Britain’s largest oil field could be producing millions of barrels a day by the autumn if Ed Miliband approves the North Sea project, its co-owner has said.

Ithaca Energy, which owns the Rosebank site off Shetland, on Wednesday said it was poised to get oil flowing within months but the Energy Secretary first needed to sign off on the plan.

Rosebank, 60 miles west of Shetland, is the North Sea’s largest undeveloped site, which could generate up to 500 million barrels of oil and gas.

However, Mr Miliband has been a fierce critic of the plan, branding it climate vandalism.

He now faces an energy crisis and growing political calls to approve such schemes, piling pressure on him to approve Rosebank.

Donald Trump, the US president, again lashed out at Britain on Tuesday for not taking more advantage of the North Sea, having previously urged the UK Government to restart drilling.

Ithaca and Rosebank’s other co-owner Adura have invested more than £3bn in the project with a giant production vessel, the Petrojarl Rosebank, now rounding Africa and due to reach Scottish waters next month.

Ithaca described Rosebank as “entering the final stages of development towards first production in 2026-27” in its latest financial results on Wednesday.

It also announced plans to start producing oil and gas from Cambo, another controversial oil and gas field almost as large as Rosebank, and from Tornado, a mainly gas field. They too will need Mr Miliband’s consent.

Yaniv Friedman, Ithaca’s executive chairman, said: “In the West of Shetland, we saw material project activity, including at Rosebank where we are progressing in line with our development timeline towards first production in 2026-27.

“We also saw significant progress on the maturation of the Cambo and Tornado projects, with all activities supporting a potential final investment decision within 12 months.”

Oil from Rosebank would be exported because the UK now lacks the refinery capacity to process it. But it would generate UK jobs and billions in taxes as well as boosting north European energy supplies.

Rosebank’s gas output would enter UK pipelines, amounting to around 1pc of national needs. The Cambo and Tornado fields lie nearby and their gas output would also enter UK pipes.

Mr Miliband would be likely to face a backlash from Labour backbenchers and climate campaigners should he approve them. All already have licences, granted by the previous government, but lack final development consents.

The decision is meant to be made on technical rather than political grounds – Adura and Ithaca must show the field meets new criteria on overall emissions from producing and using the oil and gas.

Rising pressure on Miliband

However, the looming local and regional elections in May, including for the Welsh and Scottish parliaments, also mean any decision will be carefully timed for maximum political advantage.

Andrew Bowie, the shadow secretary of state for Scotland, called on Mr Miliband to approve such projects.

Mr Bowie said: “Thousands of jobs and millions of pounds in revenue are being missed out on every day that goes past while Ed Miliband allows his eco zealotry to trump the national interest.

“The crisis caused by the closing of the Straits of Hormuz and wider impact on oil and gas exports from the Gulf demonstrate more than ever before the necessity of maxing out our domestic supply of energy from the North Sea.”

Ithaca, majority owned by Israel’s Delek and Italy’s Eni, is now one of the UK’s largest offshore operators, but has been hit hard by the North Sea windfall levy which has pushed industry taxes on oil and gas profits to 78pc.

It made pre-tax profits of $840m (£629m) but was hit by the levy and again by Labour’s decision to extend it from 2028 to 2030 – forcing it to set aside an extra $327m.

Iain Lewis, Ithaca’s chief financial officer, said the company had been hit by a tax rate of 110pc resulting in a post-tax loss of $84m.

Separately, Norwegian state-controlled energy giant Equinor has announced a string of new oil and gas discoveries in Norway’s North Sea “Sleipnir” area, close to the UK border, from which gas is piped to Britain.

Cecilie Rønning, from Equinor, said: “Sleipner is an important hub for gas exports, and we must do everything we can to identify the remaining resources. We plan to drill three additional exploration wells and two new production wells in the area this year.”

data.title: Terrible reviews for my new book have made me a better person
data.description:
I knew that my new book about female freedom would ignite disagreement. Good Slut, its title, is triggering for a good many. But molten contempt?

The Lefty female reviewerati are a feisty lot. Having kept somewhat to the side of the fiery bear pit of feminism for the last few years, I was perhaps ill-prepared for finding myself, even briefly, in its bloodbath.

But I was still knocked sideways by the sheer nastiness of the first review, by Sarah Ditum in The Spectator. Part of the trenchant “gender critical” crew, Ditum is one of those women whose knee-jerk reaction as a “feminist” is to fall right into the cliché of catfight, going straight for the low blows, and the jugular, when she doesn’t like an argument or, God forbid, a book.

We’re on the same team really. But then off she goes, shooting from what struck me as a rather Freudian hip, lambasting my “scattershot” ideas, “endemically sloppy” text, alleged errors (the example given was her error, actually), and my apparent incontinence as both a writer and thinker. She might as well have called me a hysteric. She certainly made no concession to the possibility I did know what I was writing about, given that I wrote an Mphil, PhD and an academic tome (given five stars, no less, by The Telegraph’s Tim Stanley) in this area.

She called me “intellectually dishonest and politically unserious” and concluded, “if this is the best defence that the sexual revolution can muster, the future looks dry indeed.”

To get this kind of vengeful walloping felt shocking, and not nice at all, especially as someone who – for all that I write about controversial matters – really detests personal conflict.

I don’t expect good reviews or agreement. But to feel the fury of girl-on-girl nastiness is rather shocking if you’ve generally stayed out of it. Still, through the nasty feelings, reminiscent of being back in the schoolyard, I felt interest: Ditum appeared to be doing what men have done for time immemorial, namely erasing and trampling on a woman’s attainments by making her out to be a mad illogical fool because they don’t agree with, or feel threatened, by what she is saying.

As the days trickled by, I rallied. I began to see that I’d poked a bear that hates being poked. Instead of worrying that perhaps I really am a terrible fraud, I remembered that other readers enthused and did not find the book “dishonest”, “lazy”, “sloppy” or “fatuous”.

I knew there was a discontented review in The Observer, but I gave it a wide berth once I’d determined it wasn’t a hatchet job. A glowing review came out in The Telegraph from the estimable free-thinker Ella Whelan. The largely enjoyable demands of motherhood took precedence.

But, sitting in the Green Room at Jewish Book Week, after a lovely long discussion with Michael Gove, my eye was caught by a post on X by one of those Bright Young Men of journalism, whom I considered a colleague and acquaintance. Above another vicious, somewhat messier karate chop in the New Statesman, the wunderkind snarled that my book looked absolutely “risible”. Good lord! What had I ever done to the magazine, apart from write for it and engage politely with its editors at parties?

A battle-worn matriarch sharing the Green Room told me, with love, to “toughen up”. I vowed to do so.

I’ve learnt a few things this week. One is that I do need to toughen up. The other is that when you write a book with bold ideas you soon discover that in journalism, you really do have no idea who your friends are and who can’t wait to stab you full frontally.

‘Good Slut: How Money, Sex and Power Set Women Free’ by Zoe Strimpel is out now

data.title: Millions of households face higher water bills to meet rising debt costs
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher water bills after five of Britain’s biggest suppliers were allowed to raise charges to help fund debt costs.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said it had decided to allow Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water to increase customers’ bills by an extra 2.2pc on average.

This is on top of previous bill increases allowed by Ofwat. The water regulator already approved a 24pc increase to annual water bills by the five utility firms between late 2024 and 2030, with the firms having appealed to be allowed to push through steeper rises.

The CMA’s approval will mean even sharper increases in water bills for 14 million people.

Some households are facing steeper rises than others. South East Water, for example, has been allowed to raise bills by an extra 4pc to £284 a year by 2030, while Anglian Water can increase them by an additional 2pc to £602.

The CMA said the increases reflected “the higher cost of financing which makes investment more costly to secure for water companies compared to Ofwat’s price review”.

Water companies in England and Wales are struggling with heavy debts.

South East Water’s operating company has a debt pile of £1.3bn, which is more than four times its annual revenue. Anglian Water’s debt surpassed £7.7bn at its last annual results.

Southern Water’s owner pumped hundreds of millions of pounds into the utility firm last July to see off a debt crunch.

The utility firms have argued that they need to be able to secure extra investment to “meet their environmental and drinking water quality legal obligations” and to help boost investor returns “to reflect the risks involved”.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said it had “allowed limited extra funding where that’s genuinely needed, balancing concerns about affordability with the need to secure our water supplies and cut pollution”.

‘These people should be in jail’

It follows mounting criticism from customers over the service water companies provide, as well as their pollution records.

In December, 24,000 homes served by South East Water in Tunbridge Wells were left without drinkable water for nearly two weeks.

Southern Water, which also argued that it needed to raise bills, recorded 269 pollution incidents in 2024. In 2021, the company was hit with a £90m fine for dumping billions of litres of raw sewage into protected seas.

Feargal Sharkey, a clean water campaigner, said: “What’s clear is that every household in the country is simply being abused and extorted by the greatest crime gang in history, the water industry and our government does nothing.”

Mr Sharkey added: “These people should be in jail. They should not be allowed to fleece customers even further.”

Since 1989, when the water companies were privatised, they have paid out £78bn in dividends.

A spokesman for South East Water said: “The decision to appeal was a difficult one, but one we took in the interests of our customers to ensure we were appropriately funded to deliver the infrastructure required to tackle the challenges faced in the South East.”

Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water were contacted for comment.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Elon Musk’s Tesla to start selling energy to British households
data.description:
Elon Musk’s Tesla has been given the green light to launch a household energy supplier in Britain despite Ed Miliband’s fierce opposition to the billionaire.

Ofgem has granted Tesla a licence allowing it to supply electricity to households and businesses across the country.

The licence, granted to its subsidiary Tesla Energy Ventures, took effect on Wednesday following a seven-month assessment by the energy regulator.

The approval paves the way for the launch of Tesla Energy, a household energy supplier that is likely to be closely integrated with Mr Musk’s electric car maker and home battery storage product Powerwall.

The company already runs an electricity provider in Texas that enables Tesla owners to charge their cars cheaply and sell any excess energy back to the grid.

But Mr Musk’s swoop on Britain will put the mercurial billionaire in direct competition with the likes of Octopus and British Gas.

The approval by Ofgem comes despite a public rebuke of the Tesla tycoon by Britain’s Energy Secretary.

Mr Miliband last year accused Mr Musk of being a key player in a network of individuals seeking to “destroy the ties that bind our communities and our way of life”.

Speaking at the Labour Party conference, he said: “We have a message for Elon Musk: get the hell out of our politics and our country.”

Tesla’s energy supplier plans have been in the works for a long time. The company was granted a separate electricity generation licence in 2020 and has since been hiring operations and policy staff to prepare for the launch of its retail service.

The EV maker applied for an electricity supply licence from Ofgem in July last year. However, it has not applied for a gas supply licence, meaning most UK households would have to choose a different gas provider.

Tesla must now comply with Ofgem’s licence conditions, including rules around treating customers fairly, financial responsibility and information provision.

The regulator will monitor compliance and has the power to fine the company or restrict its licence if it falls foul of the rules.

An Ofgem spokesman said: “Protecting consumers and maintaining a secure, fair energy market underpins every licensing decision we make.

“Following a detailed assessment, Ofgem has concluded that Tesla Energy Ventures Limited meets all statutory requirements to be licensed as an energy supplier and will be subject to the same binding obligations, monitoring and enforcement as all other suppliers from day one.

“We monitor compliance continuously and will not hesitate to use our powers where standards are not met.” 

Tesla, which has sold more than a quarter of a million cars in the UK, is well-placed to shake up the energy market.

In addition to its cars, the company has sold tens of thousands of Powerwall batteries to British homes, while it also sells home chargers for electric cars.

This gives the company a large existing customer base to tap into. It could also seek to capitalise on so-called “vehicle to home” technology currently under trial that uses a car’s battery to power the home.

But the launch also comes amid a sales slump for Tesla, which is grappling with softening demand and fierce competition from cut-price Chinese rivals.

The company has also suffered from a consumer backlash against Mr Musk over his support for Donald Trump, his work on the White House’s now-disbanded Department of Government Efficiency (Doge) cost-cutting project, and his enthusiasm for far-right political figures, including Germany’s AfD and Tommy Robinson.

Tesla sold 2,208 vehicles in the UK last month, down more than 45pc from a year earlier, according to data from New Automotive.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband urged to open Britain’s biggest oil field to avoid energy crisis
data.description:
Ed Miliband has been urged to approve extraction at Britain’s biggest undeveloped oil field to prevent an energy crisis.

Factory bosses and unions said the Energy Secretary should approve production at Rosebank in the North Sea after the Iran war sent energy costs spiralling.

They are also urging Mr Miliband to approve production at Jackdaw, an undeveloped gas field in British waters that could start supplying the country as soon as this summer.

It is understood Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, is separately pushing for North Sea assets to be unlocked.

Make UK, the manufacturers’ lobby group, said factories will struggle to survive more cost pressures as oil prices hover around $100 per barrel. They are urging the Government to drill the North Sea to avoid putting Britain on an accelerated path to de-industrialisation.

Stephen Phipson, the chief executive of Make UK, said: “Manufacturers are calling for the Government to act quickly to progress with the Rosebank and Jackdaw developments to mitigate energy costs and energy security because of the conflict in the Middle East.

“Historically high industrial energy costs are already preventing growth in UK manufacturing.”

Unite, which represents thousands of oil and gas workers, also urged Labour to unlock the North Sea.

Sharon Graham, the general secretary of Unite, said: “The government’s position on oil and gas is putting jobs and national security at risk.

“Blocking oil and gas production in the North Sea, especially now is simply an act of monumental political self-harm. Domestic gas from Jackdaw and oil from Rosebank are essential for jobs and for energy security.”

Factories are under pressure from soaring energy costs at a time when Mr Miliband is poised to decide whether to allow production from Shetland’s Rosebank oil field.

The oil field is the North Sea’s largest undeveloped site, which could generate up to 500 million barrels of oil. Jackdaw is mainly gas and could provide 6pc to 7pc of UK production.

The Energy Secretary will risk the wrath of Labour backbenchers and climate campaigners if he waves extraction through.

However, the sudden surge in oil and gas prices after Donald Trump launched his bombardment of Iran on Feb 28 could prove existential for British industry.

Mr Phipson said: “The recent developments in the Middle East add huge pressures to the sector and risk accelerating de-industrialisation.

“Whilst manufacturers are leading the way in renewable energy we must ensure the sector survives in the medium term. Ensuring the UK has access to its own energy reserves is now vital.”

Mr Miliband told the BBC: “New exploration licences in the North Sea, which some people are calling for, will not take a penny off people’s bills.”

Rosebank is a reserve 60 miles west of Shetland, buried 5,200ft beneath the seabed. Experts believe it could generate up to 500 million barrels of oil.

Meanwhile, the Jackdaw gas field lies in the North Sea, 150 miles east of Aberdeen. It has already been partly drilled.

If Jackdaw is approved, it could come online rapidly to support the UK’s gas supplies and potentially impact prices. Gas could enter UK pipes from the field sometime from the summer onwards.

The UK buys its gas, oil and refined products on global markets at global prices but increased supplies of pipeline gas would reduce reliance on expensive imports of LNG.

‘A rotten deal’

The calls for Mr Miliband to issue final consents for oil and gas production at Rosebank and Jackdaw prompted an immediate backlash from green groups.

Tessa Khan, the executive director at Uplift, said: “If the conflict in Iran and soaring oil prices isn’t enough to remind us of the financial pain from remaining hooked on fossil fuels, Rosebank is a rotten deal for the UK. The rig was made in Dubai, not Scotland. The oil belongs in part to the Norwegians, who will take most of the profits.

“It will do nothing to lower energy bills – it is predominantly oil for export – and the tiny amount of gas it contains is enough to reduce the UK’s import dependency by just 1pc. It is oil for profit, at our expense.”

The Conservatives, when in power, had granted the state-owned Norwegian oil company permission to drill at Rosebank, but the Scottish Court of Session overturned that decision in January last year. The court also blocked production at Jackdaw, a gas field in the North Sea.

This was on the basis that the projects’ carbon emissions had not been properly accounted for. Equinor resubmitted an application to produce oil and gas at Rosebank last October. Rosebank and Jackdaw are already licenced fields but they need the consent of the Energy Secretary.

data.title: Rothschild banking dynasty cashes out of The Economist
data.description:
Lynn Forester de Rothschild, a British philanthropist, has struck a deal to sell her stake in The Economist to a little-known Canadian billionaire.

Lady de Rothschild and her banking dynasty have agreed to sell a 26.9pc holding in the magazine to Stephen Smith, a financial services tycoon whose net worth is estimated at $7bn (£5.2bn) by Forbes.

The value of the stake was not disclosed, but was previously reported to be worth as much as £400m.

The deal marks the biggest shake-up of The Economist’s ownership since 2015, when British education publisher Pearson sold the majority of its 50pc stake alongside its sale of The Financial Times.

Those shares were bought up by Exor, the holding company of Italy’s carmaking dynasty, the Agnellis, which is now the largest shareholder in the magazine with a 43pc stake.

Lady de Rothschild’s stake in The Economist was held through EL Rothschild, a holding company she established in 2003 with her late third husband, Sir Evelyn de Rothschild, whom she married in 2000.

The American-born businesswoman hired advisers at Lazard last year to explore a sale after reportedly pushing for higher returns. The decision to cash in her 20pc holding prompted her family to follow suit.

Lady de Rothschild, who made her fortune in the telecoms industry after founding US broadband firm FirstMark Communications in 1995, is now in a relationship with Lord Saatchi, the 79-year-old advertising guru and former Conservative Party chairman.

The divestment comes at a critical time for The Economist, which was founded in 1843 by James Wilson, a Scottish hat manufacturer, to promote free trade.

Its philosophy is one of editorial independence, and its constitution bans any individual or organisation from owning a majority share, while the editor is appointed by trustees and it does not use bylines.

The weekly title is based in London but has expanded internationally and now employs 1,540 people in 26 countries.

The Economist reported revenues of £369m and a profit of £48m in the year to the end of March 2025. Its subscriber numbers increased by 3pc to 1.25 million.

But the title is now facing searching questions about its future in the digital age. The magazine has begun to shed its traditional anonymous style in favour of personality-led journalism by mounting a major push into video content.

Its political stance as a socially liberal, pro-free-trade title has also come under pressure in an era of growing polarisation.

Zanny Minton Beddoes, who has been editor of The Economist since 2015, extended her tenure by a further two years in October, breaking the tradition of editors holding a 10-year term.

Patrick Foulis, the magazine’s foreign editor, who was widely viewed as heir apparent, stepped down abruptly in the wake of the announcement.

Mr Smith has bought the near 27pc stake through Smith Financial Group, the family holding company he runs.

The 74-year-old tycoon, who grew up in Ottawa but was educated at the London School of Economics, has remained largely out of the spotlight in his home country.

He began his career flipping houses in Toronto in the 1980s before rising interest rates and a string of bad deals forced him into bankruptcy.

In 1988, Mr Smith and his partner started First National Financial Corporation, a mortgage lender that has grown to a market value of more than $2bn.

Mr Smith, who also holds major stakes in Canada Guaranty Mortgage Insurance Company and Equitable Bank, is known for his philanthropy.

He donated more than $100m to Queen’s University Canada, while he is chairman of Historica Canada, a charity dedicated to promoting the country’s history and citizenship.

A spokesman for Mr Smith told Bloomberg: “This investment reflects Mr Smith’s full support for The Economist’s long‑standing tradition of rigorous editorial independence and will see The Economist’s strategy and operations continue unaffected.”

A spokesman for The Economist said: “We can confirm that Stephen Smith, together with his family holding company, Smith Financial, has entered into an agreement to acquire a 26.9pc minority stake in The Economist Group from existing shareholders, Lady Lynn Forester de Rothschild, her family and her family foundation, subject to certain closing conditions.”

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: Seven pubs close for every vape shop opened in past decade
data.description:
Seven pubs have closed for every new vape shop that has opened in the last 10 years, research shows.

Labour has been urged to take action after warnings that an explosion of “dodgy shops” on high streets was contributing to a “breakdown in community life”.

Since 2016, the number of vape and tobacco stores has risen by 245 to nearly 2,200, according to analysis by the Centre for Social Justice (CSJ) think tank.

Meanwhile, Britain is thought to have lost almost 1,800 pubs and bars, equivalent to around seven for every vape shop opened.

The Telegraph has been campaigning to save Britain’s locals, which are reeling from rises in business rates, National Insurance contributions and the minimum wage.

In January, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor of the Exchequer, was forced to announce an emergency support package for pubs facing the twin challenges of cuts to Covid-era relief and a wave of revaluations in April, which are set to send bills soaring.

Since 2010, Britain has lost 6,900 pubs and bars, while 25,000 more barbers, nail salons and other beauty outlets have sprung up across the country, the CSJ has found.

The think tank released its analysis after a study warned that Labour would be “washed away in a tide of discontent” at the next general election unless it tackled the decline of the UK’s town centres.

Research by the University of Southampton found that people felt high streets had declined more than any other part of their area over the past decade, owing to the rise of shoplifting and collapse of well-known retailers.

Improving shopping streets was the third most important local issue for voters, behind good healthcare and reducing crime, according to YouGov polling.

Earlier this month, Steve Reed, the Communities Secretary, said people were “sick to death” of vape shops and mini-marts taking over high streets.

In an interview with The Telegraph, he pledged to clamp down on shops that “turn out to be fronts for organised crime” and encourage “real local businesses” to return to town centres.

Josh Nicholson, the CSJ’s head of housing and communities, said: “Britain’s high streets are changing and not for the better.

“The explosion of ‘dodgy shops’, shoplifting and anti-social behaviour across town and city centres points to a breakdown in community life that must urgently be addressed.

“The Government must identify the gaps in powers and resources that leave these problems unresolved and ensure that the upcoming High Streets Strategy contains a plan to clean up the social breakdown wrecking our communities.”

A government spokesman said: “Pubs are the heart of our communities, which is why we’re taking action to protect them. We’re giving communities the power to buy local pubs at risk of closing through our Pride in Place programme, which is backed by more than £5bn, and putting money directly into local people’s hands to invest in the places they love.

“This is alongside cutting business rates for pubs and bringing in new licensing laws for vape shops to make sure our high streets flourish.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Oil crisis could hand Reeves almost £3bn tax windfall
data.description:
Rachel Reeves will bank almost £3bn in extra tax revenues if the war in the Middle East leads to a sustained increase in oil and gas prices, new analysis shows.

Soaring energy costs are likely to increase the corporation tax paid by North Sea firms by £1.5bn alone, according to Granville Park Partners.

Using the Office for Budget Responsibility’s (OBR’s) own rules of thumb, the consultancy added that a windfall tax on energy companies’ profits would boost the Treasury’s coffers by a further £1.3bn if current oil and gas prices are sustained.

It comes as pressure mounts on the Chancellor to help struggling households with their energy bills.

Cornwall Insight predicted last week that energy regulator Ofgem’s price cap was on track to rise by more than 10pc in July to £1,827 from £1,641 in April.

On Monday, Sir Keir Starmer unveiled a £53m emergency fund for “vulnerable” families using heating oil. Many rural households are not protected by the price cap because they are not connected to the main gas grid.

‘Completely unacceptable’

The Prime Minister warned that Labour would not tolerate price gouging, adding that he had issued a “legal direction” to energy companies to pass on savings from government policies.

Sir Keir said: “I simply will not allow companies to make huge profits from the hardship of working people.

“That kind of conduct is completely unacceptable, so if the companies have broken the law, there will be legal action.”

Granville Park Partners, led by Adam Smith, Jeremy Hunt’s former chief of staff, said oil prices – currently trading above $100 a barrel – were much higher than the $63 assumed by the OBR for 2026.

“Relative to the March 2026 forecast, higher energy prices could increase receipts by around £2.7bn per year for every year prices remain elevated at today’s levels,” he said.

However, Mr Smith added that higher oil prices would also negatively hit the public finances.

Soaring energy costs are set to pile pressure on Ms Reeves to increase public spending budgets which are set in cash terms, and potentially could force higher spending on welfare payments, which are usually uprated each year in line with the cost of living.

Factory bosses have called for Ed Miliband to approve extraction at Rosebank, Britain’s biggest undeveloped oil field, to prevent an energy crisis.

The Energy Secretary is also facing demands from unions and the manufacturing sector to approve production at Jackdaw, an undeveloped gas field in British waters that could start supplying the country as soon as this summer.

‘Opportunity foregone’

Martin Beck, the chief economist at WPI Strategy, said the potential boost to the public purse and economy from North Sea oil and gas was huge.

He said: “The fact that oil and gas could still generate that kind of revenue, even with North Sea output languishing at multi-decade lows, says a great deal about the scale of the opportunity the UK has forgone.

“By blocking new North Sea licences, banning fracking and generally making life harder for domestic producers, policymakers have denied the country one of its clearest defences against an energy price shock.”

Rosebank, which could generate up to 500 million barrels of oil, is the North Sea’s largest undeveloped site. Jackdaw is mainly gas and could provide between 6pc and 7pc of UK production.

Mr Beck said: “More UK oil and gas production would not have stopped global prices from rising. But it would have meant more jobs, more investment and, crucially, far higher tax receipts when the Treasury needed them most.

“That would have given the Government more firepower to help households and businesses, strengthened the public finances and kept borrowing costs lower than otherwise.

The OBR currently expects the Treasury to raise £4.1bn in taxes from the oil and gas sector this financial year.

data.title: Oxford start-up backed by CIA plots British Starlink rival
data.description:
An Oxford start-up backed by the CIA is preparing to build hundreds of satellites a year as it seeks to rival Elon Musk’s Starlink.

OpenCosmos, a British satellite-maker backed by investors including the CIA’s venture capital fund, announced plans on Monday for a new orbital network called ConnectedCosmos – which it said would counter Europe’s “over-reliance on transcontinental mega-constellations”.

Rafel Jorda Siquier, the founder and chief executive, said the start-up would soon have capacity at factories including its main facility in Harwell, near Oxford, to make nearly 200 satellites per year.

“We have the ability to deliver up to 150, soon close to 200, satellites per year,” he said. “In Harwell in particular, we now have capacity for manufacturing on average a satellite every three days.”

Mr Siquier said the start-up could provide a sovereign alternative for European governments and telecoms companies, which until now have been forced to turn to Mr Musk’s Starlink for critical communications.

Starlink, which is made up of thousands of small satellites orbiting the world, has been widely used to support the conflict in Ukraine by providing connectivity to front line forces and the government.

However, officials in Europe have become increasingly wary of relying on Starlink, a division of SpaceX, because of the mercurial nature of Mr Musk.

Mr Siquier said: “It’s very important that we don’t depend on systems where decisions are being taken by a single individual on the other side of the Atlantic.

“We want to be there as a very reliable solution. Of course for the UK and of course for across Europe – and also to countries that want to partner and collaborate without having to depend on the US or China.”

Europe has been eyeing building its own sovereign satellite network, called Iris 2, but it is expected to cost billions of euros and not be ready until 2030.

In January, OpenCosmos secured key spectrum rights for low-orbit satellites from regulators in Liechtenstein that could allow it to compete with Mr Musk’s SpaceX. The licence gives it the right to launch up to 288 satellites for its network by a deadline of 2028.

“It took many by surprise we had reserved this spectrum that is worth billions,” Mr Siquier said.

Mr Siquier announced OpenCosmos’s plans for its new satellite network at Mobile World Congress, a gathering of telecoms industry leaders in Barcelona.

Separately Vodafone struck a deal with Jeff Bezos’s satellite company that will help improve mobile coverage in remote, rural areas.

The telecoms giant will use Amazon Leo’s network of satellites in low Earth orbit to help connect calls, texts and data to mobile masts in areas where it is too difficult or expensive to install fibre cables, which usually underpin networks.

In addition to tackling mobile “not-spots” in rural areas, Vodafone will also use the technology to improve network resilience for emergency calls and critical online services if fibre cables are damaged or affected by flooding.

The partnership will be rolled out across Europe, including the UK, from this year. It will also be deployed in Africa.

OpenCosmos launched the first satellites that will form part of its new network in January in partnership with New Zealand launch business Rocket Lab.

Last week, SpaceIntelReport disclosed that OpenCosmos was seeking to raise up to $200m (£160m) to fund its expansion plans.

data.title: £50bn construction giant abandons London stock market
data.description:
Building materials giant CRH is to quit the London Stock Exchange (LSE) in a further blow to Britain’s standing as a global financial hub.

CRH, which is worth nearly £54bn, said it had decided to exit the UK market because it was hamstrung by high regulatory costs and low levels of trading activity.

The firm’s shares will now be traded only in New York, where they had been listed in addition to London since September 2023.

The move serves as yet another setback for London’s struggling stock market, with CRH joining a string of high-profile companies that have defected to New York in search of higher valuations.

Online payments company Wise, worth £12bn, last year transferred its main share listing to the US, saying the move would allow better access to the world’s most liquid stock market.

Meanwhile, AstraZeneca said in September that it would upgrade its presence on the New York exchange in what bosses described as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

While the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain based in the UK, the announcement has stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker and most valuable business could ultimately use the move as a stepping stone to quitting the LSE.

CRH had already declared New York as its primary listing and had been removed from the FTSE 100 index.

The company, which is based in Dublin, said a review launched last month had concluded that quitting London completely was “in the best interests of CRH and its shareholders”.

It said: “CRH carefully considered, among other factors, the level of trading activity for its ordinary shares on the LSE as well as the additional cost, regulatory and administrative obligations arising from retaining the LSE listings.”

The firm said it had asked the Financial Conduct Authority to cancel its London listing, with the move expected to be completed by Apr 20.

CRH said on switching to the dual listing three years ago that the decision had also been partly driven by a US push for onshoring manufacturing activity, something that has gathered pace under Donald Trump.

CRH, which was founded in 1970 via a merger of two Irish cement and quarrying companies, already counts North America as its biggest market.

data.title: End of the line for supermarket barcodes
data.description:
Barcodes have begun disappearing from supermarket shelves as retailers trial technology set to replace them.

The zebra-striped labels are in the process of being phased out in favour of new, square-shaped QR codes in a move that could put an end to squinting at tiny product labels and accidentally buying food past its sell-by date.

As well as scanning the “2D barcodes” to pay, shoppers could soon scan the codes on their smartphones to pull up information about items in their basket, including dietary guidance, allergen details and expiry dates.

The new QR codes are currently being trialled at Tesco stores across the country on packets of lemons and limes, steaks and sausages, where scanning the black square gives customers access to recipe inspiration and storage instructions. Other major brands adopting the 2D barcodes include Unilever, Procter & Gamble and L’Oréal.

In the Tesco trial, some products have already had their traditional barcodes replaced entirely by QR codes, offering an early glimpse of how the technology could eventually replace the old system.

Transforming the way we shop

GS1, the non-profit organisation that oversees the world’s barcodes, is encouraging all UK retailers to adopt the new system by 2027, while experts believe the technology could transform the way we shop.

“I think it will end up being as significant as the original introduction of the barcode,” said Lee Metters, business development director at Domino Printing Sciences, a global printing manufacturer that provides equipment to produce the new labels.

Regular shoppers are well acquainted with the frustrations of traditional barcodes: the dreaded “item not recognised” message at self-checkouts, trying to flatten a crumpled voucher so it scans, or waiting for a cashier to help.

The next-generation QR codes not only scan more easily, reducing self-checkout frustrations, but can also provide shoppers with a wealth of information about their food at the touch of a button.

While navigating supermarket aisles, customers will be able to scan items with their smartphones to see where products were grown, their carbon footprint and customer reviews, without having to peer at the fine print on the label.

Help reduce waste

Currently, the main beneficiaries are supermarkets themselves, which can input use-by dates and batch numbers into QR codes. This allows them to stop the sale of out-of-date products and better predict when food will expire, helping reduce waste.

However, according to Camilla Young, programme lead for the next generation of barcodes at GS1 UK, “the potential for consumers is huge”.

With 51 per cent of shoppers having bought out-of-date food at some point - costing UK consumers £11.8bn a year - the new barcodes could eliminate the risk by flashing up warning alerts, she said.

In the future, they could even be used to track all the items in a fridge, notifying families when supplies are running low, suggesting recipes and flagging when products are close to expiring.

For people with allergies, the ability to scan food with an app rather than searching supermarket shelves for products that will not trigger a reaction would be a “game changer”, said Mr Metters.

Unilever has also been trialling accessible QR codes that allow visually impaired shoppers to scan products from a distance and receive increasingly detailed information through headphones as they move closer to the item.

Making shopping more convenient

“It’s all about making shopping more convenient,” said Mr Metters.

Barcodes were first introduced in Britain in 1979, when a box of Melrose tea bags was scanned at a Keymarkets supermarket in Spalding, Lincolnshire.

Invented in the late 1940s by US science graduates Norman Joseph Woodland and Bernard Silver, traditional barcodes contain 13 digits and hold only seven pieces of basic information – a product’s name, manufacturer, type, size, weight, colour and price.

When scanned at the till, the barcode number is matched with a vast product database to ensure customers are charged the correct amount.

Though rudimentary by today’s standards, barcode lines can be arranged to register up to ten trillion different products and are scanned worldwide around 10 billion times a day.

In contrast, QR codes, invented by Japanese scientists in the 1990s, can contain up to 4,000 characters, allowing retailers to embed far more information.

Growth during pandemic

QR codes are a type of 2D barcode and grew rapidly in popularity during the pandemic, when they were widely used to allow customers to view menus and order table service at restaurants and pubs.

Since then, they have become a common feature alongside traditional barcodes on supermarket products, often linking to promotions and loyalty schemes, with almost half of British retailers updating their tills to read them.

The difference between the latest 2D barcodes and earlier versions is that the new ones hold more information and use a standardised “language”, meaning manufacturers, retailers and shoppers can all access the same data.

They also perform the same role as a conventional barcode, meaning only one square code is needed on packaging, saving space.

“QR has been around for years, but it really started gaining public adoption during Covid. That was when people saw the opportunity to develop the digital link standard,” said Ms Young.

Mr Metters said: “Those old barcodes will still be hanging around on cupboard shelves for a good few years, but my prediction is the new ones will catch on rapidly.”

data.title: Close Brothers to cut 600 jobs amid warnings of ‘wipeout’ from car finance scandal
data.description:
Close Brothers has unveiled plans to cut 600 jobs, less than 24 hours after a short-seller warned that the UK bank could face a £1.2bn hit from the car finance scandal.

Bosses revealed the planned redundancies in half-year results on Tuesday, claiming the cuts will save £85m by 2027 as the company relies increasingly on AI and offshoring.

The latest update comes after shares in the merchant banking group fell by as much as 18pc on Monday in the wake of a short-sellers’ report.

Viceroy Research accused the company of having “substantially misrepresented” how much it will be forced to pay out to compensate drivers who were treated unfairly.

Viceroy, which gained fame for its damning reports about collapsed German payment processor Wirecard, claimed its estimates showed the merchant bank will face redress payments of between £572m and £1.2bn. This would be significantly higher than its existing £300m provision.

The short-seller warned that the payments would put Close Brothers at risk of regulatory intervention for falling below minimum capital requirements and would force it into a restructuring that would leave shareholders “substantially wiped out”.

Viceroy, which said it had taken a short position on Close Brothers, claimed that the company had not increased its current level of provision, as this would put it at risk of collapse.

British banks are facing a bill of around £11bn to compensate 14 million customers who were mis-sold car loans in deals dating back to 2007.

The Financial Conduct Authority found that lenders had broken rules by failing to properly inform customers of the commission paid to car dealers who sold them loans.

This meant that many motorists were not able to negotiate or find a better deal and may have paid a higher interest rate on their loan as a result.

The City watchdog is expected to finalise the details of the redress programme in the coming weeks, with lenders to be granted an implementation period of up to five months once the scheme is launched.

Viceroy claimed that while Close Brothers represented only a small fraction of the motor finance market, its exposure to the redress scheme was “structurally and proportionally far higher” than its rivals.

It said this was because the merchant bank was among the “earliest and most aggressive adopters” of the so-called discretionary commission arrangements (DCA) model, which allowed car dealers to earn higher commissions when they sold more expensive loans to customers.

Close Brothers initially recognised a £165m provision in relation to the scandal before increasing it to £300m last October. However, it has warned that the ultimate cost could be materially higher or lower.

Viceroy described Close Brothers an “outlier” among other lenders that face repayments. Lloyds has set aside almost £2bn to cover costs, while Santander and Barclays have warned they face hits of around £460m and £325m, respectively.

Close Brothers, which traces its history to 1878, has already struck deals to sell off key subsidiaries to strengthen its balance sheet before repayments.

A spokesman for Close Brothers said: “Close Brothers strongly disagrees with the report, which relates to the company’s approach to provisioning in respect of motor finance commissions and resulting impact on its capital position.

“Our provisioning approach in relation to this matter is in accordance with UK-adopted international accounting standards and follows a robust governance process.”
